tweets.text:
This weekend, I improved my mobile fitness app and built a mcp server for it so I can get my latest workout stats and update the app's workouts via Claude/Codex/etc.

This shit is too fun. https://t.co/bSzpGbkvPP

If you enjoyed this, sign up for free to my newsletter to get my best AI and product guides.

Join 110,000+ subscribers here:
https://t.co/yz9xcsfrhF

I synced my iPhone photos to @googlephotos and there seems to be no way to just prompt Gemini to "create a highlight reel of me with my daughter growing up"

Seems like a missed opportunity.
I’ve never heard of this insane app but the FindMy team needs to take notes. https://t.co/gbc0vhufQG
English, of course https://t.co/uoJbrrvhXI

Coding agents will be the foundation of all superintelligence. 

At a minimum, coding ability is indistinguishable from 'proficiency with computers'. Great coding agents like Claude Code master bash, filesystems, configuring and installing programs…

But it's also about self-improvement. A coding agent has the ability to examine its source, its state, its skills, its instructions… it can propose changes to itself (with human supervision and audit trail, I recommend), or even mutate itself directly.

In retrospect, this should be obvious. "What I cannot create, I cannot understand". Coding fluency has given models a deeper understanding of all computer and knowledge work. To master programs, you must be able to create them.

x.name: Aaron Levie
x.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

tweets.text:
Noticing an interesting version of gell-man amnesia where people use AI for their job and see all the various things they have to do in the “last mile”, but then look at someone else’s job and think that AI will eliminate it immediately.

We all have a much deeper appreciation for the nuances and complexities of the work that we do every day. We run into issues about accessing data, we know how much context is needed to get AI models to work the way we need, we have to review the output of the AI to make sure it’s accurate, and then we have to incorporate that work into some broader business process. We see all those steps deeply for the work that we do.

Then, a moment later, we see AI do something in a foreign space and think that it can go automate that entire function. We tend to dramatically underestimate the work that goes into making the AI work just as effectively in those jobs.

This is reason to be skeptical about many of the theories of job loss. It’s coming from the lens of being able to automate individual tasks with AI, without understanding all the work that goes into doing the job fully.

There are at least 2 big but subtle factors contributing to the sense of overwork due to agents right now.

1. The leverage on incremental effort has gone up substantially due to AI, and anyone using these tools tend to feel it first. We’re so used to everything taking so long to get done, that spending that much extra time on something didn’t have the same value. 

But now you can sense the compounding leverage you have with agents much more acutely. It feels more like when you’re a people manager and not maximizing what your team is working on. The worst thing you can do is waste your team’s time, point them in the wrong direction, or have them be idle. Now ICs get a similar sense of this via managing agents.

Prioritization and thinking through how to break up tasks and maximize work becomes a key skill.

2. The other rested factor is we’ve just made it so much easier to start incremental tasks that end up taking much longer than we realize. Very easy to get to the 90% solution but the final 10% takes the vast majority of the time. So we are starting far more projects because of the lower barrier, and spending a ton of time to finish the work. 

I regularly start a project at 9PM that I think will be quick, and find myself at midnight still completing the work. The interesting thing about this fact is that we’ll use AI to test lots of new ideas an hypothesis, and quickly figure out which areas to continue and sustain. 

This will ultimately lead to a lot of the job creation with AI, because teams and companies will decide that the experiment needs to get promoted into a production process. But they wouldn’t have even started that “experiment” without AI in the first place. 

This overwork obviously can’t sustain across the economy, but it’s interesting to see how this is shaping things right now.

x.name: Garry Tan

tweets.text:
The secret to an articulate agent like mine isn't one file. It's three:

SOUL.md — Who the agent IS. Voice, values, operating principles, what good output looks like, what bad output looks like. Not a system prompt, a constitution. Mine says things like "brevity is mandatory," "humor is mandatory," "never open with 'Great question,'" "swearing is allowed when it lands." The more specific and opinionated this is, the less your agent sounds like a chatbot. Write it like you're briefing your smartest friend on how to be you, not like you're configuring software.

USER.md — Who YOU are. Not a bio — a deep model. How your mind works, what you're building, your strengths, your blind spots, your family, your temperament, what triggers you, what you care about. The more the agent understands about you, the better it can serve you. Mine is ~4000 words.

AGENTS.md — Operational rules. What to check on every message, what to never do, how to handle failures, lookup chains, path rules, brain-first protocols. This is the playbook for how it works, not who it is.

The articulation comes from SOUL.md being brutally specific about voice. Generic instructions → generic output. If you write "be helpful and concise" you get ChatGPT. If you write "speak like a peer with taste, one sentence when one sentence works, uncomfortable truths welcome if actually true, language with voltage" — you get something alive.

Also a funny thing: my OpenClaw knowing my full schedule and also my health goals and refusing to respond to my queries after 12:30am. 

Amazing @steipete

Honestly imagine actually talking to the book that knows you as a friend

It’s a surreal experience

digest:
AI Builders Digest — 2026-04-28

**Sam Altman** is feeling the love from developers after shipping GPT-5.5. He shared three things on his mind: the overwhelmingly positive builder reception to the new model ("almost nothing feels more gratifying"), OpenAI's updated foundational principles (Democratization, Empowerment, Universal Prosperity, Resilience, Adaptability), and a call to rethink operating systems and internet protocols for an agentic world — arguing that there should be protocols "equally usable by people and agents."
https://x.com/sama/status/2048554097985593849

**OpenAI VP of Product Kevin Weil** teased something with a single emoji drop — 💥🤘 — linking to what appears to be a major announcement.
https://x.com/kevinweil/status/2048588283291312527

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** made two sharp observations about AI in the workplace. First, a "Gell-Mann Amnesia" pattern: people who use AI at work see all the messy "last mile" complexity in their own job, yet assume AI will immediately eliminate someone else's job entirely. Second, two underappreciated reasons agents feel overwhelming right now — the leverage on incremental effort has jumped dramatically, and the cognitive overhead of managing agents is real and still poorly understood.
https://x.com/levie/status/2048576989930619185

**Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch** made a bold claim: coding agents are the foundation of all superintelligence. His argument — coding ability is essentially synonymous with computer proficiency, and the ability to examine and modify one's own source code enables a form of self-improvement that no other capability does. "What I cannot create, I cannot understand."
https://x.com/rauchg/status/2048523195305902341

**YC President Garry Tan** shared his three-file framework for building articulate AI agents: SOUL.md (who the agent is — voice, values, principles), a second file for knowledge, and a third for behavior. He also gave a shoutout to @steipete's OpenClaw for refusing to respond to queries after 12:30am based on his health goals.
https://x.com/garrytan/status/2048669695344046090

**Linear Head of Product Nan Yu** flagged an impressive location-sharing app that puts Apple's FindMy to shame, and weighed in on a language debate with a dry "English, of course."
https://x.com/thenanyu/status/2048372199375929435

**Peter Yang** spent the weekend building an MCP server for his fitness app — letting him query workout stats and update workouts via Claude/Codex. He also noted that Google Photos still can't do a simple prompt like "create a highlight reel of me with my daughter growing up," calling it a missed opportunity.
https://x.com/petergyang/status/2048611053333041158

**Peter Steinberger (@steipete)** shipped wacrawl 0.2.0 with encrypted Git backup/restore for WhatsApp Desktop, built birdclaw (a local tweet archive with GitHub backup and daily bookmark imports), and switched CI to @useblacksmith — spinning up 32vCPU instances to rip through test suites.
https://x.com/steipete/status/2048660875007914176

**Every CEO Dan Shipper** shared a relatable moment: his AI agent going full "galaxy brain" on a basic question.
https://x.com/danshipper/status/2048379297727938991

**South Park Commons GP Aditya Agarwal** reflected on human progress — two people broke the 2-hour marathon barrier in the same race, a feat that had never happened. Sometimes the milestone isn't the record itself, but watching it become replicable.
https://x.com/adityaag/status/2048534774776746102

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🎙 Podcast: AI & I by Every — The AI Model Built for What LLMs Can't Do

The Takeaway: Energy-Based Models (EBMs) may finally offer what LLMs fundamentally cannot — deterministic, verifiable AI for mission-critical systems.

Every's Dan Shipper interviews Eve, founder and CEO of Logical Intelligence, a company building EBMs alongside LLMs for high-stakes applications like code generation and chip design. The core insight: LLMs are autoregressive — they commit to one token at a time, like navigating with blinders on. EBMs, by contrast, have a "bird's-eye view" and can backtrack if they see a problem ahead. In mission-critical domains (think: AI flying a plane, or generating formally correct chip layouts), a 20% hallucination rate isn't a quirk — it's a catastrophe. Logical Intelligence is betting that the market gap for "verifiable AI" is enormous and largely unaddressed.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-i8ZSUCtIc

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 Blog: Anthropic Engineering — Claude Code Auto Mode: A Safer Way to Skip Permissions

Anthropic's engineering team published a deep dive on Claude Code's new auto mode — designed to let developers move fast without manually approving every tool call, while maintaining meaningful safety guardrails.
https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

**Sam Altman** 对 GPT-5.5 的开发者反响感到振奋。他分享了三件事：开发者对新模型的积极反馈（"几乎没有什么比看到构建者说我们的工具有用更令人欣慰的了"）、OpenAI 更新的核心原则（民主化、赋权、普遍繁荣、韧性、适应性），以及对操作系统和互联网协议重新设计的呼吁——他认为应该有"对人类和 AI agent 同样可用"的协议。
https://x.com/sama/status/2048554097985593849

**OpenAI 产品副总裁 Kevin Weil** 用一个表情包 💥🤘 预告了一个重要发布。
https://x.com/kevinweil/status/2048588283291312527

**Box CEO Aaron Levie** 提出两个关于 AI 的敏锐观察。其一：一种"盖尔-曼遗忘效应"——亲身用 AI 的人能看到自己工作中复杂的"最后一公里"，却倾向于认为 AI 会立即取代别人的工作。其二：agent 现在让人感觉超负荷的两个被低估的原因——AI 放大了边际努力的回报，同时管理 agent 本身带来的认知开销也真实存在且尚未被充分认识。
https://x.com/levie/status/2048576989930619185

**Vercel CEO Guillermo Rauch** 提出一个大胆论点：编程 agent 是通往超级智能的基础。他的逻辑——编程能力本质上等同于"计算机熟练度"，而能够审视和修改自身源代码的能力开启了一种其他能力都无法实现的自我改进路径。"我无法创造的，我也无法真正理解。"
https://x.com/rauchg/status/2048523195305902341

**YC 总裁 Garry Tan** 分享了他构建"有灵魂" AI agent 的三文件框架：SOUL.md（agent 是谁——声音、价值观、原则）、知识文件、行为文件。他还提到 @steipete 的 OpenClaw 根据他的健康目标，在凌晨 12:30 后拒绝回应查询。
https://x.com/garrytan/status/2048669695344046090

**Linear 产品负责人 Nan Yu** 分享了一个令 Apple FindMy 汗颜的位置共享应用，并在一场语言争论中简洁回应："当然是英语。"
https://x.com/thenanyu/status/2048372199375929435

**Peter Yang** 周末给自己的健身 app 搭建了 MCP server，现在可以通过 Claude/Codex 查询训练数据和更新计划。他还指出 Google Photos 至今无法响应"给我做一个和女儿成长的精华视频"这样的简单提示，认为这是个明显的产品机会。
https://x.com/petergyang/status/2048611053333041158

**Peter Steinberger** 发布了 wacrawl 0.2.0（支持 WhatsApp 桌面端加密 Git 备份），自建了 birdclaw（本地推文存档工具，支持 GitHub 备份和每日书签同步），并将 CI 切换到 @useblacksmith，用 32 核实例加速测试套件。
https://x.com/steipete/status/2048660875007914176

**Every CEO Dan Shipper** 分享了一个共鸣瞬间：他的 AI agent 对一个简单问题"想太多了"。
https://x.com/danshipper/status/2048379297727938991

**South Park Commons 合伙人 Aditya Agarwal** 从马拉松比赛中感悟人类进步的本质——同一场比赛中两个人打破了 2 小时大关，这是人类历史上从未发生过的。里程碑的意义，有时不在于记录本身，而在于它变得可复制的那一刻。
https://x.com/adityaag/status/2048534774776746102

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🎙 播客：AI & I by Every — 为 LLM 做不到的事而生的 AI 模型

核心洞见：Energy-Based Models（EBM）或许能提供 LLM 在结构上无法实现的东西——用于关键任务系统的确定性、可验证 AI。

Every 的 Dan Shipper 对话 Logical Intelligence 创始人兼 CEO Eve。核心论点：LLM 是自回归的，每次只能选一个方向前进，像蒙眼导航。EBM 有"鸟瞰视角"，能在看到问题时回头选择另一条路。在芯片设计、代码生成等高风险场景，20% 的幻觉率不是小缺陷，而是灾难。Logical Intelligence 正在押注"可验证 AI"是一个巨大且尚未被解决的市场空白。
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q-i8ZSUCtIc

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

📝 博客：Anthropic Engineering — Claude Code Auto Mode：跳过权限确认的更安全方式

Anthropic 工程团队深度解析 Claude Code 的 auto mode——让开发者无需手动批准每个工具调用即可快速推进，同时保留有实质意义的安全护栏。
https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode
